coredata.dc:title: CQDs improved the photoelectrocatalytic performance of plasma assembled WO3/TiO2-NRs for bisphenol A degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yi
Ma, Hao
Chen, Xinwei
Wang, Wenbin
Li, Fangying
Qiang, Tao
Shen, Yiping
Cong, Yanqing
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been supported on WO3/TiO2-NRs using a hydrothermal method and a novel CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs composite formed via dielectric barrier discharge. The composite electrodes were characterized using morphology, structural, optical and electrochemical analysis. The CQDs were successfully prepared on the composite electrode with the highest photocurrent density reaching 2.51 mA·cm–2 under UV–visible light irradiation (100 mW·cm–2) and an applied voltage of 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode exhibited a good degradation effect toward bisphenol A (BPA) (75.66 %) combined with the production of hydrogen (0.89 mmol) in Na2SO4 system after 2 h of the photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) reaction and the BPA degradation rate reached 100 % after 7 min of reaction in both simulated and real seawater. The CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs exhibited excellent stability and efficient PEC performance in which the CQDs acted as electron reservoirs to capture and promote charge separation. Our analysis of intermediates of BPA degradation indicated the possible degradation pathways that mainly formed BPA polymers in the Na2SO4 system or chlorinated compounds in the high chloride salt system.

head.ce:title: CQDs improved the photoelectrocatalytic performance of plasma assembled WO3/TiO2-NRs for bisphenol A degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been supported on WO3/TiO2-NRs using a hydrothermal method and a novel CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs composite formed via dielectric barrier discharge. The composite electrodes were characterized using morphology, structural, optical and electrochemical analysis. The CQDs were successfully prepared on the composite electrode with the highest photocurrent density reaching 2.51 mA·cm–2 under UV–visible light irradiation (100 mW·cm–2) and an applied voltage of 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode exhibited a good degradation effect toward bisphenol A (BPA) (75.66 %) combined with the production of hydrogen (0.89 mmol) in Na2SO4 system after 2 h of the photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) reaction and the BPA degradation rate reached 100 % after 7 min of reaction in both simulated and real seawater. The CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs exhibited excellent stability and efficient PEC performance in which the CQDs acted as electron reservoirs to capture and promote charge separation. Our analysis of intermediates of BPA degradation indicated the possible degradation pathways that mainly formed BPA polymers in the Na2SO4 system or chlorinated compounds in the high chloride salt system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA), an endocrine disruptor, improves the mechanical properties of polymers, such as hardness, transparency and weight. BPA can also be used as a stabilizer or antioxidant for various plastics. Despite these advantages, it also has adverse effects that endanger human health in at concentrations of < 1 mg·m–3 (Bhadra et al., 2018). BPA can be detected in tap water, surface water, wastewater treatment plant effluent and landfill leachate at concentrations in the range of 120–300 mg·L–1 (Colin et al., 2014; Huang et al., 2012; Yamamoto et al., 2001). It has been reported that the leaching rate of BPA from plastics followed the order of: seawater > river water > deionized water, which is due to the effect of sodium ions in seawater (Liu and Aydil, 2009). At the same time, there are antioxidant components present in seawater, which resulting in the slow natural degradation of BPA in seawater. BPA leached from plastic fragments will become a serious problem in the marine environment. Therefore, reducing or removing BPA in polluted water is extremely important for the efficient use of water resources.

The methods used for of BPA removal including biological, physical, chemical, and advanced oxidation technologies, which are gradually being developed by several researchers (Bai et al., 2022; Gan et al., 2021; Kyrila et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2010). Photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) process, which combines photocatalytic (PC) and electrocatalytic (EC) process and exhibits high efficiency, non-toxic and no secondary pollution, and is considered as an attractive strategy for the degradation of organic matter and hydrolysis to produce oxygen and hydrogen (Fan et al., 2016; Goulart et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019; Xiang et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2016). In addition, the types of electrolytes used, such as Na2SO4 and NaCl, can influence the PEC performance toward the degradation of BPA. A higher concentration of chlorine ions has a quenching effect for·OH and competes with the organic pollutants adsorbed on the surface of photocatalyst during the PC process (Brugnera et al., 2010; Li et al., 2019). Therefore, an external bias applied during the photocatalytic process may promote the reaction of chloride ions with the active species to produce active chlorine species (RCS), including HClO/ClO–, Cl2 ·–, ClO·and Cl·, to enhance the oxidation capacity for pollutant removal (Ma et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2019). The research and development of efficient and useful photocatalysts has been the focus of researchers in order to reduce the adsorption of chlorine species on the catalyst surface, and at the same time, PEC technology has been used to improve the conversion of the active ions.

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) and tungsten trioxide (WO3) are representative catalysts in the field of photocatalysis due to their stable chemical properties and excellent optical properties, which are used in photoelectrocatalytic system. The photocatalytic performance is also affected by the morphology of TiO2 and WO3. Quantum dots, weir structures, tubular, nanoflakes and rod materials with higher specific surface area can provide more active sites for catalytic reactions and accelerate electron transport (Feng et al., 2008; Kong et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018; Pan et al., 2017; Sayama et al., 2010; Song et al., 2012). The WO3 and TiO2 heterojunction can broaden the spectral absorption range to the visible light region for efficient photocatalytic degradation and membrane filtration (Zhao et al., 2021). As the conduction band of WO3 is more positive than TiO2, electron transfer to WO3 is difficult for superoxide radical anion (O2 •–) formation and remains in WO3/TiO2 system (Dozzi et al., 2016). The PEC performance of WO3/TiO2 increases the transport of the photogenerated electrons to the external circuit (Hunge, 2017; Xin et al., 2014). In order to facilitate photogenerated electron-hole pair separation, ternary composites synthesized using various modified methods such as semiconductor composite materials, heterojunction formation and metal/non-mental deposition, have been were explored (Li et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2021). Among them, the deposition of quantum dots (QDs) with zero-dimensional nanostructures and the quantum confinement effect is one of efficient modification methods used to enhance photogenerated electron transfer (Chen et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021a). Liu et al. (2018) deposited WO3 QDs on GC/TiO2 to enhance the photocatalytic performance in order to utilize the excellent spectral response range of the WO3 QDs. Chen et al. (2019) prepared GO/WO3 QDs/TiO2 @SiO2 microspheres, which exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance when compared to GO/TiO2 @SiO2 and TiO2 @SiO2. Huang et al. (2021) reported that the combination of WO3 QDs with g-C3N4 boosts charge separation for broadband photocatalysis.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have excellent optical, biological and electron transfer properties, and are usually used as a co-catalyst in optoelectronic reactions. When compared with a TiO2/Ti electrode, the CQDs decorated electrode can enhance the removal efficiency of methylene orange during PEC (Niu et al., 2020). Wang et al. (2022) loaded CQDs as a co-catalyst on NiTiO3 (NTO) photocatalyst to promote the water oxidation reaction, which improved the catalytic performance 7-fold when compared with the original NTO catalyst. Zhou et al. (2021b) synthesized a CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode, which showed a 2.98-fold higher photocurrent than FeOOH/BiVO4 due to the significant enhancement in light absorption in the UV and visible region by the CQDs. Jamila et al. (2020) prepared a WO3/GO/NCQDs ternary photocatalyst exhibiting higher methyl orange degradation efficiency than WO3/GO and WO3. Su et al. (2020) showed that CQDs decorated TiO2/g-C3N4 exhibited the highest 1,4-dioxane removal efficiency during PEC. It can be predicted that the deposition of CQDs on the WO3/TiO2 heterojunction can form a strong electron transfer process and promote electron-hole separation at the interface.

In this study, CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrodes were successfully fabricated using dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) plasma deposition and their degradation effect toward BPA and hydrogen production evaluated in a Na2SO4 system. In addition, BPA removal in simulated seawater used as a high chloride salt system with the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode was also analyzed. Furthermore, total organic carbon (TOC) and GC-MS analyses were also performed and a mechanism for the degradation of BPA discussed.

Fluorine-doped tin oxide conductive glass (FTO) was used as the catalyst carrier. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36 %), tetrabutyl titanate (C16H36O4Ti, 99 %), sodium tungstate (Na2WO3, 99 %), oxalic acid (H2C2O4, 99 %), isopropanol (C3H8O, 99 %), citric acid (C6H8O7, 99 %) and ammonium hydroxide solution (NH3(aq), 28 %) were used as received without any purification.

Fig. 1 shows a flow chart for the catalyst preparation process. The first step involves preparation of the precursor solution. A total of 40 mL of HCl and 40 mL of distilled water were stirred well. After 10 min, 1.2 mL of butyl titanate was added and stirred until completely dissolved. The FTO conductive substrate was placed side up in the hydrothermal reactor containing the precursor solution and then heated at 170 °C for 4 h to obtain the TiO2-NRs. Next, 1 mmol of H2C2O40.2 H2O, 0.5 mmol of Na2WO40.2 H2O and 15 mL of deionized water were mixed, isopropanol (35 mL) and hydrochloric acid (1.8 mL) added dropwise to the resulting solution and heated hydrothermally at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain WO3 as a yellow solid. The TiO2-NRs were then spread over a quartz dish and a 0.5 g·L–1 solution of WO3 was added to the dish connected with the high voltage of the DBD and then discharged at an input voltage of 25, 35, 45 and 55 V with an input power of 10.6, 14.5, 30.1 and 41.3 W for duration of 5, 10, 15 and 20 min, respectively (Fig. S1). After drying at room temperature, the WO3/TiO2-NRs composite electrode was obtained. The prepared WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode was then reacted at 160 °C for 4 h in the presence of a citric acid solution (42.0 g·L–1) and the pH adjusted to 6 using ammonia solution. The CQDs were formed on the WO3/TiO2-NRs to obtain the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs composite electrode.

A Sigma 300 field emission scanning electron microscope was used to analyze the surface morphology of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs. X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS; ThermoFisher, Waltham, MA, USA) was performed to analyze the surface oxidation states of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs. The crystal structure was detected using X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Smartlab 9 kW diffractometer. The mineralization rate of pollutants was obtained using TOC analysis.

A three-electrode system comprised of the prepared electrode, Pt sheet and Ag/AgCl was established using a CHI 660D/E workstation for electrochemical characterization. During the PEC degradation process, a dual-electrode system containing the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs as the anode and Ti sheet as the cathode was used in a working volume of 100 mL under UV-Vis irradiation (100 mW·cm–2). Na2SO4 (0.1 M) and simulated seawater (2.5 % NaCl, 1.1 % MgCl2, 0.40 % Na2SO4 and 0.16 % CaCl2) was used as the electrolyte containing different concentrations of BPA. For the same reaction, the hydrogen (H2) produced by the cathode was collected and analyzed using gas chromatography (GC).

BPA was detected using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) at a wavelength of 278 nm using a binary mixture of methanol and distilled water (typically 6:4 v/v) as the mobile phase. Active chlorine was determined using spectrophotometry to analyze the color at a wavelength of 515 nm (Ma et al., 2021). H2 was detected using GC with nitrogen used as the carrier gas at 0.1 MPa, no shunt, bridge flow of 60 Ma, oven temperature of 70 °C, injection port and detector temperature of 120 °C. The byproducts of BPA were detected using gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (7890A-5977B, Agilent, USA) on a HP-5MS column.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode are shown in Fig. 2. The prepared TiO2-NRs were homogeneous and dense providing a large surface area and number of loading sites along with a nanoflower-like structure, in which the nanorod diameter was ∼100 nm and height was ∼2 µm. WO3 has a hollow nanosphere structure with diameter of 2 µm composed of nanosheets of 50–100 nm thickness as seen from the individual broken spheres with more active sites for catalytic reactions and enhancing light reflection and diffusion (Jing et al., 2021). No morphological differences were observed for the loaded CQDs due to their small size (Tian et al., 2015). EDS mapping maps show that C, Ti, O, and W elements were uniformly distributed in the electrodes, indicating the successful preparation of CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrodes. In addition, the SEM images of CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs after reaction were depicted that the structures of hollow nanosphere WO3 and nanotubes TiO2 have not changed. It was probably due to the DBD deposition of WO3 on TiO2-NRs tightly. The relative elemental compositions of CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs before and after reaction detected by EDS were shown in Fig. S2. The relative weight and atomic of CQDs were changed a little indicating small amount of carbon reduction.

As shown in Fig. 3(a), the fragments structure of TiO2 nanorods and WO3 nanosheets were observed as hollow sphere structure of WO3 was composed of nanosheets. Fig. 3(b) and (c) shows that TiO2 (110) and WO3 (002) were detected according to a lattice spacing of 0.350 and 0.381 nm, respectively (Jing et al., 2021; Su et al., 2020). Fig. 3(d) shows the TEM images of the CQDs loaded on TiO2-NRs with a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm for (002) (Cao et al., 2019; Kong et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2021b) and a diameter of ∼9 nm, which was consistent with the literature (Su et al., 2020), indicating that the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs were successfully prepared.

W, C, Ti and O were observed in the XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 4. The peaks associated to Ti corresponding to Ti4+ 2p3/2 and Ti4+ 2p1/2 were observed at 458.8 and 464.2 eV, respectively (Fu et al., 2015). The O 1 s spectrum exhibits a peak at 529.7 eV corresponding to the lattice oxygen atoms in WO3 and a peak at 531.6 eV attributed to the surface adsorption of O2 and H2O (Wang et al., 2016). The W 4 f spectrum showed two peaks corresponding to W 4 f7/2 and W 4 f5/2 of W6+ centered at 35.4, 35.2 and 37.5 eV (Daniel et al., 1987), respectively. The C 1 s spectrum showed a peak at 284.8, 285.4, and 288.1 eV attributed to CC, C-OH and CO (Jing et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018), respectively.

The XRD patterns obtained for the TiO2-NRs, WO3/TiO2-NRs and CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrodes (Fig. S3) exhibit peaks at 36.0, 41.2, 54.3, 62.7 and 69.7° that correspond to the (101), (111), (002), (112) and (211) crystal planes of rutile TiO2 indicating that rutile TiO2 was synthesized and stable with no change in its crystal structure observed after plasma discharge. Upon analyzing the XRD patterns obtained for WO3/TiO2-NRs and QDs/WO3/ TiO2-NRs, it can be concluded that the characteristic peaks corresponding to the (002) and (340) crystal planes of WO3 (JCPDS No. 20-1324) were observed at 2θ = 34.0 and 62.1°, respectively (Rezaei et al., 2011), indicating that WO3 was successfully loaded onto the electrode. The XRD patterns obtained for the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs were not significantly different from those of WO3/TiO2-NRs due to the small amount of CQD supported on WO3/TiO2-NRs, and the loading of CQDs not changing the crystal form of WO3/TiO2-NRs (Su et al., 2020).

Fig. 5(a) shows that a heterojunction structure was formed between WO3 and TiO2 on the WO3/TiO2-NRs after DBD deposition, which improves the absorbance in the visible light region. The absorbance intensity of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode in the visible light region was significantly improved, which may be due to the CQDs promoting electron transfer and up-conversion effects. The band gap energy calculated using Eg = 1240/λ0 (where λ0 represents the absorption threshold). The λ0 of TiO2-NRs, WO3/TiO2-NRs and CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs were 409, 413 and 450 nm, respectively. The CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs exhibit a narrower band gap (Eg = 2.75 eV) than TiO2-NRs and WO3/TiO2-NRs, which can utilize visible light more efficiently.

Fig. 5(b) shows the photoluminescence analysis of CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs, WO3/TiO2-NRs, TiO2-NRs to reveal the effect of CQDs for photo-induced electron-holes separation. The binary composite of WO3/TiO2-NRs has lower PL luminous peak than TiO2-NRs indicating the heterojunction structure enhanced the electrons and holes separation. And ternary composite of CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs has the lowest PL luminous peak, showing the CQDs inhibit the recombination rate of pairs of electrons-holes of WO3/TiO2-NRs efficiently.

Fig. 6(a) and (b) depicts that the effects of the operation discharge input voltage and operating time for the electrode. When the input voltage and operating time increased, the optoelectronic properties of the electrodes initially increase and then decrease. The optimum conditions for the optoelectronic properties were a discharge input voltage of 35 V and discharge time of 10 min Fig. 6(c) depicts the photocurrent density of the composite electrode increases significantly upon loading WO3 and CQDs onto the TiO2-NRs. The maximum photocurrent density was 2.51 mA·cm–2 at bias voltage of 0.6 V, which is 2.4-fold higher than that observed for WO3/TiO2-NRs (1.03 mA·cm–2) and 15-fold higher than that observed for TiO2-NRs (0.16 mA·cm–2). The photocurrent of CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs was relative higher than reported composites under UV-Visible light irradiation (Kong et al., 2019; Lai and Sreekantan, 2013; Liu and Perng, 2020; Yuan et al., 2020). Fig. 6(d) shows the on/off transient photoresponse curves, which show that the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs have a stable photocurrent response at 1.23 V vs. RHE for H2 production. The photocurrents of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs were 23-fold higher than that observed for TiO2-NRs and 3.2-fold higher than WO3/TiO2-NRs, respectively.

Fig. S4 shows the M-S test and Nyquist curves obtained for the different electrodes. The flat-band potential of bare TiO2-NRs was − 0.48 V. Upon the deposition of WO3, the flat-band potential moves to − 0.46 V, which is similar to that reported in a related study (Zhao et al., 2021). When the CQDs and WO3 were deposited onto the TiO2-NRs, the flat band potential was observed at most negative − 0.50 V, which resolve the problems of heterojunction of WO3/TiO2 difficult for O2•- formation and photoelectron remains in WO3/TiO2 system. Moreover, the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode had the smallest resistance radius among the electrodes studied. This indicates that the internal impedance of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode was small and exhibits high charge separation and transfer efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC activity in a Na2SO4 system
Effect of the operating parameters on the degradation of BPA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S5 (a) and (b) show the effect of different operation input voltages and discharge times used to deposit WO3 on the composite electrodes. The removal of BPA using the WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode modified using a discharge voltage of 35 V reached a maximum removal efficiency of 60.10 % with an optimal deposition time of 10 min

The decomposition rate for BPA using the PEC system increases as the initial concentration of BPA decreases (Fig. S5 (c)) and the maximum removal efficiency was reached at a potential bias of 3 V (Fig. S5 (d)). When the potential bias was > 3 V, the oxygen evolution reaction could be a competitive response. It was observed that the lower the initial pH, the higher the rate of BPA decomposition and that acidic condition was more efficient than alkaline condition, as shown in Fig. S5 (e). The reason for this phenomenon may be the facilitated charge transfer between the photoelectrode and electrolyte under acidic conditions (Bian et al., 2019). In addition, the changes in the pH and conductivity of the treated solution were subtle in Fig. S5 (f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The PEC activity of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The different electrodes were used to degrade BPA in an Na2SO4 electrolyte, as shown in Fig. 7(a). The CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode for pollutant degradation reached 75.66 % after 120 min of the PEC reaction. The BPA degradation efficiency of depending on the type of PEC, EC and PC processes using the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs composite electrode is presented in Fig. 7(b). The BPA removal efficiency observed for PEC was 4.7- and 10.6-fold higher than EC (16.17 %) and PC (7.16 %) after 120 min of reaction, respectively, which was attributed to the electric bias voltage enhancing photogenerated electron transfer during the PEC oxidation process.

In Fig. 7(c), tert-butanol was used as a hydroxyl radical scavenger, which reduced the BPA degradation rate to 59.42 % after 120 min. After adding the h+ scavenger ammonium oxalate, the BPA degradation rate decreased to 65.33 %. The presence of p-benzoquinone (used to capture O2∙–) decreased the BPA PEC degradation rate to 33.89 %. These results indicated that superoxide radicals were an active species for this PEC system. To simulate an actual water body, the removal rate of BPA decreased to 45.74 % and 65.92 % in the presence of HCO3 – and humic acid (10 mmol·L–1) respectively, indicating that both interfering ions have a competitive effect on the degradation of BPA, as shown in Fig. 7(d).

Fig. 8(a) shows for the same reaction, the amount of H2 produced with CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs, WO3/TiO2-NRs and TiO2-NRs was 0.876, 0.751 and 0.171 mmol over 2 h of the PEC process, respectively. The H2 production performance of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs under PC, EC, PEC conditions and the effects of various pollutants are shown in Fig. 8(b). The catalytic activity under PC and EC conditions was low, while the catalytic activity under PEC condition was much higher, which can be attributed to the external bias voltage effectively promoting electron–hole separation and inhibit electron–hole recombination. Upon the introduction of BPA, the amount of H2 increases from 0.747 to 0.876 mmol after 120 min of reaction, indicating that the degradation of pollutants and H2 production have a good synergistic effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC activity in a high chloride salt system
PEC activity in simulated seawater system
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 shows the degradation of BPA using the different electrodes and under the different reaction processes in simulated seawater. The degradation of BPA with the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs composite electrode reached 100 % after 7 min of the PEC reaction. Under EC and PC conditions, the removal rates of BPA in simulated seawater were only 35.31 % and 11.87 % after 30 min, respectively. The treatment effect on BPA in simulated seawater was significantly higher than that observed in the Na2SO4 system. This was attributed to the oxidation of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode in simulated seawater due to the presence of chloride ions.

The concentration of active chlorine in simulated seawater system has a peak value, indicating that the active chlorine reacts to form ∙OH and Cl∙ during the PEC process, as depicted in Eqs. (1)–(5). In the BPA-containing system, the concentration of active chlorine slowly increases within 10 min of the reaction and then continued to increase after the degradation of BPA, showing its reaction with BPA. With the complete degradation of BPA, the pH drops from 7.34 to 3.56, while the conductivity was basically unchanged. This may be attributed to the number of hydroxyl radicals increasing in the PC reaction, which can promote the degradation of BPA under acidic conditions (Fang et al., 2014; Panizza and Cerisola, 2009; Wang et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of BPA in actual seawater
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10(a) shows the degradation rate of BPA in actual seawater reached 100 % after 7 min of catalytic reaction, which was similar to that observed in simulated seawater. The pseudo-first order reaction kinetic constant (k) of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode observed in simulated and actual seawater was 0.8959 min–1 (R2 = 0.9981) and 0.5930 min–1 (R2 = 0.9964), respectively, due to the effects of bicarbonate ions and other organic contaminants present in actual seawater. The degradation effect observed using different concentrations of BPA with the photoelectrode in actual seawater is shown in Fig. 10(b). The CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode has a high degradation effect for BPA concentrations in the range of 5–15 mg·L–1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability of the modified electrode for the degradation of BPA
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode as a photoanode was determined over 10 catalytic cycles performed in the Na2SO4 and simulated seawater systems. Fig. 11 shows that after 10 PEC cycles, the removal rate of BPA in the Na2SO4 electrolyte system decreased from 75.66 % to 69.06 %, and the H2 production decreased from 0.941 mmol to 0.785 mmol. The removal efficiency of BPA also remained nearly constant after 10 repeated cycles using the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs in simulated seawater. In addition, the total carbon (TC) in a 100 mL solution without BPA before and after the reaction using CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs under PEC condition was also detected to be 4.818 mg/L and 4.150 mg/L in Na2SO4 solution and 2.585 mg/L and 1.815 mg/L in simulated seawater, respectively. The CQDs loading on one CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode was ∼2.0 mg, which showed that the leaching of CQDs was ∼3.5 %.

The CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrodes exhibit changes in their chemical structure before and after the reaction using ATR-FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. S6 (a) shows an absorption peak at 806 cm–1 was generated due to the Ti-O bond (Kalaivani and Anilkumar, 2018) and the W-O-W and WO stretching vibrations led to the appearance of peaks at 648 and 966 cm–1, respectively (Daniel et al., 1987). The peak near 1428 cm–1 corresponds to the -OH groups due to surface adsorbed water. However, the characteristic peaks did not change significantly indicating the high stability of the electrode during its reuse.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode shown in Fig. S6 (b) indicates that the photocurrent density of the electrode remains essentially constant before and after the reaction. Fig. S6 (c) shows the i-t curves obtained under simulated solar irradiation with an applied bias voltage of 0.6 V. The current fluctuations when the light source was turned on were caused by the transient separation and recombination of holes and photogenerated electrons, and the photocurrent density was basically maintained at 2.45 mA·cm–2 after 1 h. The above results also indicate that the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode has good photochemical stability.

Table 1 lists the recent BPA degradation performances of different electrodes and the relevant CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrodes used for the degradation of organic substances. The CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs photoanode prepared in this study can show improved catalytic performance for the degradation of BPA under low energy consumption conditions in Na2SO4 when compared with other TiO2 or WO3 based photoanodes (Fan et al., 2016; Goulart et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019; Xiang et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2016). The prepared electrodes also exhibit a higher removal efficiency in simulated seawater when compared with TiO2 or WO3 electrodes in NaCl solution (Brugnera et al., 2010; Xiao et al., 2019). Although Cl- adsorbed on the surface of TiO2 competes with the degradation of BPA, the PEC process promotes the formation of active chlorine species that rapidly react with BPA. In addition, the relevant WO3/TiO2-based photocatalysts were compared with our study for the degradation of several types of pollutants, such as dyes, phenol, 4-nonylphenol, benzoic acid, under PC and PEC conditions (Hunge, 2017; Pinedo-Escobar et al., 2021; Rhaman et al., 2020; Xin et al., 2014), which was also similar to the degradation of BPA in our study. This shows the efficient PEC performance of CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs for the degradation of organic pollutants, which can be attributed to the good photoelectric response ability of the ternary electrode and charge transfer effect of the CQDs.

The mineralization rate of BPA during the degradation process in Na2SO4 and simulated seawater systems was determined in order to study the degradation of BPA, as shown in Fig. 12. The mineralization rate of BPA in simulated seawater after 120 min of reaction (87.58 %) was much quicker than that in the Na2SO4 electrolyte (52.17 %). This indicates that the combined action of O2∙–, ∙OH and active chlorine species leads to most of the BPA to be eventually mineralized into CO2 and H2O.

The HPLC chromatographs obtained for the degradation of BPA in the Na2SO4 and seawater systems during the PEC reaction are shown in Fig. S7. In the Na2SO4 system, no obvious intermediate products were observed during the PEC reaction, while in the seawater system, the degradation of BPA was significantly accelerated. In addition, the signal peaks of unknown intermediates M1 and M2 appeared at detection times of 3.8 and 4.5 min, and the two intermediates tended to increase and then decrease, which may be due to the participation of chloride ions and active chlorine species in the solution.

Table S1 and Table S2 show the intermediates detected using GC-MS and the possible degradation pathways of BPA are discussed in Fig. 13. In the Na2SO4 system, BPA reacts with the reactive radicals to generate BPA radical intermediates, which in turn produce BPA polymer. The BPA polymer is then attacked and broken by the reactive species to produce 2,4-di-tert-butylphenol (m/z = 206) and 2,4-dimethylphenol (m/z = 122), which are further converted into 1,2,4,5-tetramethylbenzene (m/z = 134) and 3-ethyl-o-xylene (m/z = 134). Finally, the ring-breaking effect results in the intermediates demineralizing into small molecules to achieve the final degradation products. In the simulated seawater system, reactive substances, such as reactive chlorine, chlorine radicals and hydroxyl radicals, synergistically act with each other. Firstly, BPA breaks the bond and combines with chloride ions to form p-chlorophenol (m/z = 160), followed by the rapid ring-breaking of p-chlorophenol to produce 1,1,3-trichloroacetone (m/z = 194), 2-chloro-2,3-dimethylbutane (m/z = 120), 1,1,3,3-tetrachloroacetone (m/z = 194), dichloroacetyl chloride (m/z = 146), dichloroacetic acid (m/z = 128), and 1,1,1,3,3-pentachloroacetone (m/z = 230), which are finally mineralized into CO2 and H2O.

The instantaneous photocurrent of the different electrodes was measured (Zhang et al., 2013) using the following formula:
(6) D = (It – Ist)/(Iin – Ist)
where It, Iin, and Ist correspond to the photocurrent at t (s), initial and steady time, respectively. When LnD = −0.7, the t value of the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs was 5.7-fold higher than the TiO2-NRs (Fig. S8) showing the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs enhance current-carrying electron separation. Therefore, it is feasible to deposit using DBD method to improve the performance of PEC.

Based on the above findings, the PEC reaction mechanism of the synergistic hydrogen production and BPA degradation using the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs electrode was discussed. Fig. 14(a) indicates photoinduced electrons excited by WO3/TiO2 heterojunction under UV-Vis light irradiation and transferred to CQDs to enhance the photocatalytic activity in Na2SO4 system. Up-converted PL properties of CQDs can act as electron reservoirs to capture the photogenerated electrons and further promote charge separation (Tian et al., 2015). Electrons excited from CQDs to TiO2 conduction band react with surface adsorbed oxygen to generate O2 ∙–. CQDs deposited on WO3 surface also received electrons to generate O2 ∙– (Jamila et al., 2020) to effectively degrade BPA as confirmed by flat band potential and radical capture experiment. In high chloride system, holes on the TiO2 valence band have strong oxidizing properties, part of which can directly degrade BPA or generate ∙OH with water to react with BPA and the other holes react with Cl- to generate reactive chlorine species by PEC process, as shown in Fig. 14(b). The strong oxidizing material can rapidly degrade BPA, and the photogenerated electrons move to the cathode via the applied bias to react with the adsorbed water to generate hydrogen gas. The main reaction equations are shown below (Mohamed Riyas et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2021).

A novel CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs composite electrode has been successfully prepared using DBD plasma deposition and a hydrothermal method, and characterized using SEM, EDS, TEM, XRD, XPS and ATR-FTIR. The loaded hollow spherical WO3 increases the number of active sites, which is beneficial to enhance the reflection and diffusion of light. When the DBD modification input voltage is 35 V and operation time is 10 min, the CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs composite electrode exhibited the best PEC activity with a maximum photocurrent density of 2.51 mA·cm–2.

CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs is a low cost, efficient and stable catalytic material that exhibited excellent PEC performance for degrading BPA and generating H2. BPA acts as a sacrificial agent to promote the effective H2 forming reaction during the PEC process. In addition, CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs exhibit an excellent PEC effect in simulated seawater containing a high concentration of chloride. Under the conditions of simulated sunlight irradiation with an applied bias voltage of 3 V, the degradation rate of BPA reached 100 % over 7 min, which is equally efficient in actual seawater. TOC detection showed the mineralization rate of BPA in the different systems in which the mineralization rate of BPA in simulated seawater system was significantly improved. The degradation mechanism of BPA has been discussed, which provides a novel idea for the elimination of BPA in a seawater system.

Waste products that use bisphenol A (BPA) as a plastic stabilizer or antioxidant widely exist in seawater and other water bodies. BPA as an endocrine disruptor that can improve the mechanical properties of polymers, is prone to adversely affect human health. In this study, we prepared a CQDs/WO3/TiO2-NRs catalytic material. It showed excellent PEC performance in degrading BPA and generating H2 in simulated seawater containing high chloride salts and actual seawater, significantly improving the mineralization rate of BPA, and effectively reducing the environmental load.
